moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the English Opening's hypermodern approach—we're NOT fighting for the d4 square with a pawn yet. Instead, c4 controls d5 and prepares to support our pieces from the side. This is the Réti idea: flexible, strategic play where we'll build pressure on the long diagonal and light squares rather than dominating the center immediately. This opening rewards patience and positional understanding.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c4 controls d5 and prepares to build pressure on the long diagonal and light squares rather than dominating the center immediately.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black immediately prepares ...d5, a typical Slav setup. The c6 pawn supports the d5 push and prevents us from playing d4 easily, which is exactly Black's plan. By playing ...c6 first, Black keeps the position flexible—we can't assume they'll go into a traditional Slav structure yet.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's c6 prepares ...d5, keeping the position flexible and preventing an easy d4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our knight to its ideal square, controlling d4 and e5 while preparing to support our queenside expansion. Nf3 is flexible: it allows us to support b3 and c4, and later we might maneuver the knight to d4 or e5 depending on how Black develops. This move sets us up for the Gurevich System's trademark approach.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nf3 controls d4 and e5, supporting b3 and c4 for queenside expansion.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black challenges our c4 immediately. Playing ...d5 is the principled response—Black wants central space and can later support this pawn with ...e6, creating a solid Slav formation. Black isn't worried about losing the d5 pawn because the position remains closed and they have good piece placement.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...d5 challenges c4, aiming for a solid Slav formation with ...e6, and the pawn is safe due to closed lines and piece placement.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We choose a solid, patient setup over cxd5. Playing e3 supports our d4 square and keeps the center tension, which is exactly what the Gurevich System wants. We're planning to build a slow, strategic position with pieces like Bb2, b3, and later potentially d4. This is less forcing than cxd5, but it gives us more long-term pressure on Black's position.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: e3 supports d4 and keeps center tension, building slow pressure with Bb2 and b3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops naturally, controlling the d4 square and supporting the e6 advance. Nf6 is flexible and traditional—it's the perfect square for this knight in a Slav structure. Black is calmly consolidating space and preparing to complete their setup with either ...Be7 or ...Bd6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on f6 controls d4 and supports e6, preparing ...Be7 or ...Bd6 in a Slav structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we execute the second phase of the Gurevich System: we support the long diagonal and prepare to place our bishop on b2, where it will eye e5 and d4 from afar. By playing b3, we're also signaling we're not interested in a d4 push immediately—instead, we want strategic pressure. This move is the trademark of the system and keeps the position flexible.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: b3 supports the long diagonal for a bishop on b2, eyeing e5 and d4 while keeping the position flexible.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the Slav structure with ...e6, solidifying their center and preparing ...Be7 or ...Bd6 to develop the light-squared bishop. The e6 pawn also closes the long diagonal against our future Bb2. Black now has a compact, well-supported pawn chain (c6-d5-e6) and is ready for piece development.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's ...e6 solidifies the Slav pawn chain c6-d5-e6, closing the long diagonal and preparing ...Be7 or ...Bd6.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We develop our queenside knight naturally. Nc3 supports d5 (making it harder to defend) and prepares Bb2, which will be our next move. From c3, the knight also eyes d4 and e4 later. This is the natural, straightforward development that fits the slow buildup of the Gurevich System.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 develops, supports d5, prepares Bb2, and eyes d4 and e4.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the dark-squared bishop actively to d6, where it supports the center and opens the e7 square for the rook or queen. Placing the bishop here (rather than e7) shows Black wants some activity—the bishop eyes the h2 square and puts light pressure on our position. Black has now completed a solid, flexible setup and can choose between kingside castling or further maneuvers like ...dxc4 or ...a6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's bishop on d6 eyes h2, supporting a flexible setup with options like ...dxc4 or ...a6.
